description: The figure displays two calculated phase volume fraction diagrams showing the molar amount of phases versus temperature (0–1300 °C) for Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels. Subplot (a) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-0.4 and (b) to 9Al-10Mn-1C, with legends identifying BCC_A2, FCC_A1, and KAPPA_E21. In (a), BCC_A2 dominates at low temperature (~0.88–0.92 mol), remains stable until ~700 °C, then drops sharply to ~0.52 mol near 800 °C before rising again toward 1300 °C; FCC_A1 is negligible below 700 °C but increases to a peak of ~0.49 mol around 850 °C then declines; KAPPA_E21 stays near 0.05–0.10 mol before dissolving above ~800 °C. In (b), FCC_A1 starts at ~0.80 mol and decreases markedly above 700 °C; BCC_A2 rises steeply from near zero to a maximum of ~1.0 mol around 950 °C before falling; KAPPA_E21 is stable at ~0.20 mol until disappearing near 900 °C. As discussed in the caption and text, these contrasting thermodynamic gradients explain why the 0.4C alloy undergoes austenite decomposition during tempering at 600 °C but not at 800 °C, whereas the 1C alloy shows decomposition at both temperatures. The diagrams support the core conclusion that chemical composition governs phase equilibria and thus the driving force for austenite decomposition independent of kinetic considerations.
